title: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH)
oneLiner: Euvolemic hyponatremia from unsuppressed ADH release, often due to lung/CNS pathology—key clue: concentrated urine despite low plasma osmolality.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old woman with a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to the ED with confusion and mild nausea for 3 days. She is euvolemic on exam—mucous membranes moist, no pedal edema, JVP not elevated, skin turgor normal. Her sodium is 119 mmol/L. Urine osmolality is 420 mOsm/kg, plasma osmolality 248 mOsm/kg. Urine sodium is 54 mmol/L. She takes no diuretics, and her thyroid and adrenal function are normal.

**Dissecting the pattern clues:**

- **Confusion + nausea** → symptomatic hyponatremia; brain cell swelling drives neuro symptoms
- **Euvolemic exam** → key to differential: not hypovolemic (no dry mucus, low JVP), not hypervolemic (no edema, no crackles)
- **Low plasma osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)** → true hyponatremia, not pseudohyponatremia (normal osmolality excludes hyperlipidemia/paraproteinemia)
- **High urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg)** → inappropriate ADH activity; normal response to hyponatremia is to dilute urine (urine osm <100)
- **Elevated urine sodium (>20-40 mmol/L)** → suggests ADH-driven water retention without renin-angiotensin drive; excludes hypovolemic causes (urine Na <20)
- **Smoking history** → red flag for small cell lung cancer (most common cause of ectopic ADH)
- **Normal thyroid, adrenal, renal function** → rules out mimics (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, renal failure)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Post-operative patient** with pain, nausea, and IV fluids → may have mild hypovolemia but also ADH release from stress; urine Na can be low or high. The “gray zone” – check cortisol and rehydration response.
- **Thiazide-induced hyponatremia** – thiazides impair diluting ability AND cause mild volume depletion → can mimic SIADH because urine Na may be elevated due to diuretic effect. Key clue: off thiazides and give 1-2L of 0.9% saline; if Na corrects spontaneously, it’s thiazide-induced, not true SIADH.
- **Ecstasy (MDMA) use** – drug-induced ADH release plus polydipsia from raving → profound acute hyponatremia with seizures. Urine osmolality will be very low if water intoxication alone, but moderate if ADH-driven. History and toxicology screens are crucial.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
SIADH is a condition where your body makes too much antidiuretic hormone (ADH) *despite* low blood sodium and normal blood volume. That extra ADH forces your kidneys to hold onto water, diluting your sodium down to dangerous levels—like trying to make a cup of strong tea but keeping the kettle running.

**Why this topic matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** SIADH is the most common cause of hyponatremia in hospitalized patients (~25-35% of all cases). You will see it weekly.  
- **Stakes:** Severe acute drops (<120 mmol/L) cause brain swelling → seizures, coma, respiratory arrest. But *overly rapid correction* (≥10 mmol/L in 24h) can cause osmotic demyelination syndrome—a permanent, devastating tetraplegia.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Missed diagnosis because the patient looks euvolemic but you ignore the urine lab. Or worse, you give normal saline, which paradoxically worsens SIADH (sodium load is excreted while water is retained).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**  
ADH (vasopressin) acts on V2 receptors in the collecting duct → inserts aquaporin-2 channels → water is reabsorbed from the tubule back into the bloodstream. Normally, ADH is turned off when plasma osmolality falls below ~285 mOsm/kg. In SIADH, that off-switch is broken. ADH keeps pumping water in, expanding total body water. But the retained water stays *inside* the vascular space and interstitial fluid—the patient becomes “diluted” but not volume-overloaded because the body senses the volume expansion (despite normal measured volume) and suppresses aldosterone, so retained water is gradually shunted to urine. The key: **euvolemia** – the patient does not develop edema because the retained water escapes into the intracellular space as cells swell (that's why brain symptoms occur).

**Mechanism → sign mapping:**

- **Low plasma osmolality, high urine osmolality:** This is the “trump card” – normally, low plasma osm should trigger a dilute urine (urine osm <100). A urine osm >100 in the setting of low plasma osm means ADH is present when it shouldn't be. The higher the urine osm, the more inappropriate the ADH action.
- **High urine sodium:** Because the expanded volume suppresses the renin-aldosterone system, the kidney *doesn't try to hold onto sodium*. Any sodium you eat is excreted freely. So urine Na >20-40 mmol/L is typical. If urine Na is low (<20), think hypovolemia (kidneys clinging to Na).
- **Low serum uric acid and low BUN:** Chronic water retention dilutes uric acid and BUN. Uric acid <4 mg/dL is a sensitive marker for SIADH (normalization after correction suggests SIADH was not volume-depleted).
- **Absence of peripheral edema:** The retained water distributes evenly across all compartments – intracellular swelling causes neurological symptoms but extracellular volume doesn't stretch enough to cause pitting edema (unlike heart failure or cirrhosis where Starling forces are abnormal).

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette clues (euvolemia, high urine osm, high urine Na, low uric acid) all stem from this single pathophysiological story: inappropriate ADH → water retention → dilution → volume expansion → aldosterone suppression.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Pulmonary** | Small cell lung cancer (most common cause overall) | Heavy smoker, hilar mass on CXR |
| | Pneumonia, TB, lung abscess | Productive cough, fever, lobar consolidation |
| | Positive pressure ventilation | ICU patient on ventilator |
| **CNS** | Meningitis (bacterial/viral) | Headache, nuchal rigidity, CSF pleocytosis |
| | Stroke (especially hemorrhagic) | Sudden focal deficit, CT shows bleed |
| | Head trauma, brain tumor | History of fall, CT/mass |
| | Guillain-Barré | Ascending paralysis, recent infection |
| **Drugs** | SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) | Starting or dose increase of antidepressant |
| | Carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine | Epilepsy or trigeminal neuralgia patient |
| | Vinca alkaloids (vincristine) | Chemotherapy, lymphoma patient |
| | Cyclophosphamide (high dose) | Pre–bone marrow transplant |
| | Desmopressin (DDAVP) | Patient on synthetic ADH for diabetes insipidus or enuresis |
| **Other** | Pain, stress, nausea (any cause) | Post-operative, severe pain, vomiting |
| | Idiopathic (no cause found, often elderly) | Exhaustive workup negative; may be due to reset osmostat |
| | HIV (multiple mechanisms) | HIV positive with low CD4, central lesions |

**Most common cause:** Small cell lung cancer (ectopic ADH). If a patient with SIADH is older, smoker, and has a lung lesion, assume SCLC until proven otherwise.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (Bartter-Schwartz, modified):**  
Essential for confirming SIADH. You need all of:
1. Hyponatremia (Na <135 mmol/L)
2. Low plasma osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)
3. Inappropriately high urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg, often >plasma osm)
4. Elevated urine sodium (>20-40 mmol/L on normal salt intake)
5. Clinical euvolemia (no edema, no signs of hypovolemia)
6. Normal adrenal/thyroid/renal function (exclude mimics)

**Key tests and interpretation:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Serum osmolality** | True osmotic state | Differentiates hypo-osmolar from iso/hyperosmolar hyponatremia (pseudohyponatremia) | <275 mOsm/kg = true hyponatremia |
| **Urine osmolality** | Renal concentrating ability | Confirms inappropriate ADH action | >100 mOsm/kg with low plasma osm = SIADH; <100 = primary polydipsia or beer potomania |
| **Urine sodium** (spot) | Tubular sodium handling | Distinguishes SIADH from hypovolemia | >20-40 = SIADH, <20 = hypovolemic (or low salt intake) |
| **Serum uric acid** | Water dilution effect | Sensitive marker; low in SIADH, normal/high in hypovolemia | <4 mg/dL supports SIADH; normalizes after correction if SIADH |
| **Cortisol (AM or ACTH stimulation)** | Exclude adrenal insufficiency | Adrenal crisis can mimic SIADH; also causes low Na | Random cortisol <100 nmol/L or failed synACTHen test → adrenal insufficiency |
| **TSH (T4 if needed)** | Exclude hypothyroidism | Severe myxedema can cause hyponatremia | Elevated TSH, low T4 → treat thyroid |
| **Chest imaging** | Identify pulmonary cause | Rule out lung cancer, infection | Hilar mass → SCLC; infiltrate → pneumonia |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out**

- **Confirms SIADH:** Low plasma osm (<275) **plus** urine osm >100 **plus** euvolemia (with normal cortisol and TSH). Basically, if the urine is concentrated when the blood is dilute, you've nailed it.
- **Rules out SIADH:** Urine osmolality <100 mOsm/kg (thumbs up for primary polydipsia). Or if correction of hyponatremia with 0.9% saline leads to complete resolution, it's hypovolemic (not SIADH).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – treat symptoms, not numbers:**

- **Severe symptoms (seizures, GCS <13, severe confusion):**  
   → **3% hypertonic saline** 2 mL/kg IV bolus (e.g., 100-150 mL) over 10-20 minutes. [Some guidelines use 150 mL over 15 min].  
   → Goal: raise Na by **4-6 mmol/L** in first 4 hours – enough to reverse brain swelling, but not enough to risk osmotic demyelination.  
   → Check Na after each bolus; repeat bolus if symptoms persist and rise <4-6 mmol/L.  
   → **Why?** 3% NaCl is ~513 mmol/L of sodium. It draws water out of swollen brain cells, reducing intracranial pressure quickly.  
   → **Pitfall:** Don't aim for normal Na (~135) immediately – that's dangerous. Get safe, not normal.

- **Mild-moderate symptoms (headache, nausea, mild confusion):**  
   → **Fluid restriction** (first-line for asymptomatic/mild SIADH): limit to 800-1000 mL/day total fluid intake (all sources).  
   → Not a rapid fix (rises 1-2 mmol/L/day), but safe.

**Ward (24-72h) – gradual correction, avoid demyelination:**

- **Monitor Na q6-8h.** Target correction rate:  
   - ≤10 mmol/L in first 24h  
   - ≤18 mmol/L in first 48h (some say ≤8 mmol/L per 24h in high-risk patients).  
- **If rise too fast:** Consider desmopressin (DDAVP) 1-2 mcg IV/SC to “stop the free water loss” and re-lower Na. Yes, you read that right – you can treat overcorrection by giving ADH back!  
- **Continued fluid restriction** if Na slowly improving.  
- **If no response or refractory (fluid restriction fails after 48-72h):**  
   → **Demeclocycline** 300-600 mg PO BID (tetracycline that induces nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; takes 3-5 days to work).  
   → **Vasopressin receptor antagonist (vaptans):** Tolvaptan 15 mg PO daily, titrate up to 30-60 mg. Use only if serum Na <125 and patient has no liver disease (risk of hepatotoxicity). Warn: can cause overcorrection – must monitor every 4-6 hours.  
   → **Why vaptans?** They block ADH at V2 receptor, causing aquaporin downregulation and water diuresis. Expensive, narrow indication.

**Long-term – treat the cause:**

- **If underlying drug** (SSRI, carbamazepine, etc.) → stop or switch (with advice from pharmacist/specialist).  
- **If SCLC** → chemo (resolution of hyponatremia often parallels tumor response). Temporarily use fluid restriction or vaptans.  
- **If idiopathic/reset osmostat** → long-term fluid restriction, possibly fludrocortisone (but risk of edema/hypertension).

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do NOT give 0.9% normal saline as first line in SIADH** – the sodium load (154 mmol/L) is excreted, but the water is retained, so hyponatremia may *worsen* in urine-concentrating states. Exception: if hypovolemia is part of the picture (mixed SIADH + volume depletion).  
- **Do NOT correct by >10 mmol/L in 24h** – osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) is permanent. High-risk: alcoholics, liver disease, malnourished, K+ <4.  
- **Do NOT immediately think “fluid restriction failed” after 12 hours** – it takes 24-48h to see effect. Be patient.  
- **Do NOT use vaptans in hypoalbuminemia, liver disease, or anuric kidney failure.**
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | SIADH | Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) | Hypovolemic Hyponatremia | Primary Polydipsia | Thiazide-Induced |
|---------|-------|----------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Volume status** | Euvolemic | Hypovolemic (low JVP, dry) | Hypovolemic | Euvolemic | Mild hypovolemia |
| **Urine sodium** | >20-40 | >20 (high, driven by natriuretic peptides) | <10-20 | >20 (but dilute urine) | Variable (often high due to Na loss) |
| **Urine osmolality** | >100 (often >300) | >100 | >100 (concentrating due to low volume) | <100 (dilute, <50) | >100 (if ADH also released) |
| **Serum uric acid** | Low (<4) | Low (also due to natriuresis) | Normal/high (volume contraction) | Low-normal | Variable |
| **Response to 0.9% saline** | No change or worsens | Corrects hyponatremia | Corrects hyponatremia | Corrects quickly | Corrects |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Euvolemia + high urine osm | Hypovolemia + high urine Na (often intracranial disease) | Low urine Na | Low urine osm (<100) | Recent thiazide start; resolves after stopping |

**Clinical pearl:** The hardest distinction is between SIADH and CSW in neurosurgical patients. A 2L NS challenge: if Na improves and volume normalizes, it's CSW. If Na drops or stays same, it's SIADH.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Brain swelling (cerebral edema) – acute hyponatremia**  
- **Presents:** Severe headache, vomiting, confusion, seizures, coma, respiratory arrest.  
- **Key signs:** GCS ≤12, focal neurology? (Think CVA vs. SIADH).  
- **What to do:** Hypertonic saline bolus (as above) and urgent senior review.

**2. Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) – from overcorrection**  
- **Presents:** Flaccid quadriparesis, dysarthria, dysphagia, locked-in state. Typically **2-5 days after correction**.  
- **Key signs:** Bilateral weakness + bulbar signs. MRI shows symmetric hyperintensities in pons (central pontine myelinolysis) or extrapontine.  
- **What to do:** Prevent! If overcorrection occurs (>10 mmol/L in 24h), give DDAVP 1-2 mcg IV/SQ to stop water loss, and consider re-lowering Na with D5W or free-water bolus (under expert guidance).  
- **Late-presentation pattern that tests “what if untreated”:** A chronic hyponatremic patient (Na 110-115) is corrected to 125 in 12 hours with high rate fluids → 5 days later they are bed-bound, mute, unable to swallow → ODS. Classic exam stem.

**3. Seizures**  
- From severe hyponatremia (Na <115). Treat with hypertonic saline + benzodiazepines. Avoid phenytoin if possible.

**4. Hypovolemia from hypertonic saline? (rare)**  
- Overly aggressive hypertonic saline can cause fluid overload if patient has reduced ejection fraction or renal failure. Monitor for pulmonary edema.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **SIADH causes: “I P 3 C”**  
  - **I** – Idiopathic  
  - **P** – Pulmonary (cancer, infection, vent)  
  - **3 C** – CNS (tumor, bleed, meningitis), Chemodrugs (vinca, cyclophosphamide), Common drugs (SSRI, carbamazepine)  

- **Diagnostic criteria: “L U N A R”**  
  - Low plasma osm (<275)  
  - Urine osm >100 (or >plasm osm)  
  - No edema (euvolemia)  
  - Acute? Check symptoms  
  - Renals normal – also check adrenal/thyroid  

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Uric acid is your shortcut:** If low (<4 mg/dL) in a hyponatremic patient, odds are SIADH. If normal or high, think hypovolemia or thiazides.
2. **BUN <10 mg/dL (in SI units <3.6 mmol/L)** also suggests water-diluted state.
3. **Never rely on urine Na alone** – in a low-salt diet, even SIADH can have low urine Na. Always interpret with volume status.
4. **If urine osm <100, stop everything – it's not SIADH.** It's polydipsia or beer potomania.
5. **The “stress triad”:** Post-op patient with pain, nausea, and opioids → high ADH. Often iatrogenic SIADH. Low threshold to restrict fluids.
6. **Check potassium before correcting Na** – if hypokalemic, correct K first because K deficit can mask total body Na deficit; you may overcorrect Na.
7. **In the elderly “reset osmostat” variant:** ADH turns on at a lower set point (e.g., plasma osm 260). Urine osm becomes dilute if plasma osm falls below that set point. Diagnose with water loading test (rarely needed). Treat with mild fluid restriction only.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking CSW for SIADH and fluid-restricting (causes hypovolemic shock).  
- Giving “normal saline” for SIADH as a routine – almost always wrong.  
- Discharging a patient with Na 128 and asymptomatic without plan – they may bounce back with seizures if they drink too much.  
- Failing to check cortisol in SIADH – missing adrenal insufficiency is a killable mistake.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / MET team:**

- **Symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, GCS <13, severe confusion)** – call ICU/high dependency for hypertonic saline infusion and monitoring.
- **Na <115 mmol/L** even if asymptomatic (high risk of seizure, needs monitored setting).
- **Correction rate >10 mmol/L in 24h** – call renal/endocrinology senior for guidance on DDAVP re-lowering.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (especially if respiratory rate >25, O2 sats <91%, systolic BP <90) – escalate as per local policy.
- **Lactate >2** (if hypovolemic component or sepsis).
- **GCS drop ≥2 points** from baseline.
- **Any evidence of osmotic demyelination** (triad: dysarthria + quadriparesis + altered mental status days after correction) – urgent neurology/MRI.

**Specific thresholds to act on:**

- Na <120 + any neurological symptom → immediate hypertonic saline (see management)
- Na <125 and rapid fall (e.g., drop >0.5 mmol/L/hour) → consider ICU if symptomatic
- K+ <3.5 mmol/L in a hyponatremic patient (must correct K first to avoid overcorrection of Na)
- In patient on vaptans: check LFTs before starting and at 1 week; if ALT >3x ULN, stop vaptan and call senior.

rawMarkdown:
## SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH)

**One-liner:** Euvolemic hyponatremia from unsuppressed ADH release, often due to lung/CNS pathology—key clue: concentrated urine despite low plasma osmolality.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old woman with a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to the ED with confusion and mild nausea for 3 days. She is euvolemic on exam—mucous membranes moist, no pedal edema, JVP not elevated, skin turgor normal. Her sodium is 119 mmol/L. Urine osmolality is 420 mOsm/kg, plasma osmolality 248 mOsm/kg. Urine sodium is 54 mmol/L. She takes no diuretics, and her thyroid and adrenal function are normal.

**Dissecting the pattern clues:**

- **Confusion + nausea** → symptomatic hyponatremia; brain cell swelling drives neuro symptoms
- **Euvolemic exam** → key to differential: not hypovolemic (no dry mucus, low JVP), not hypervolemic (no edema, no crackles)
- **Low plasma osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)** → true hyponatremia, not pseudohyponatremia (normal osmolality excludes hyperlipidemia/paraproteinemia)
- **High urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg)** → inappropriate ADH activity; normal response to hyponatremia is to dilute urine (urine osm <100)
- **Elevated urine sodium (>20-40 mmol/L)** → suggests ADH-driven water retention without renin-angiotensin drive; excludes hypovolemic causes (urine Na <20)
- **Smoking history** → red flag for small cell lung cancer (most common cause of ectopic ADH)
- **Normal thyroid, adrenal, renal function** → rules out mimics (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, renal failure)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Post-operative patient** with pain, nausea, and IV fluids → may have mild hypovolemia but also ADH release from stress; urine Na can be low or high. The “gray zone” – check cortisol and rehydration response.
- **Thiazide-induced hyponatremia** – thiazides impair diluting ability AND cause mild volume depletion → can mimic SIADH because urine Na may be elevated due to diuretic effect. Key clue: off thiazides and give 1-2L of 0.9% saline; if Na corrects spontaneously, it’s thiazide-induced, not true SIADH.
- **Ecstasy (MDMA) use** – drug-induced ADH release plus polydipsia from raving → profound acute hyponatremia with seizures. Urine osmolality will be very low if water intoxication alone, but moderate if ADH-driven. History and toxicology screens are crucial.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
SIADH is a condition where your body makes too much antidiuretic hormone (ADH) *despite* low blood sodium and normal blood volume. That extra ADH forces your kidneys to hold onto water, diluting your sodium down to dangerous levels—like trying to make a cup of strong tea but keeping the kettle running.

**Why this topic matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** SIADH is the most common cause of hyponatremia in hospitalized patients (~25-35% of all cases). You will see it weekly.  
- **Stakes:** Severe acute drops (<120 mmol/L) cause brain swelling → seizures, coma, respiratory arrest. But *overly rapid correction* (≥10 mmol/L in 24h) can cause osmotic demyelination syndrome—a permanent, devastating tetraplegia.  
- **Common pitfalls:** Missed diagnosis because the patient looks euvolemic but you ignore the urine lab. Or worse, you give normal saline, which paradoxically worsens SIADH (sodium load is excreted while water is retained).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
ADH (vasopressin) acts on V2 receptors in the collecting duct → inserts aquaporin-2 channels → water is reabsorbed from the tubule back into the bloodstream. Normally, ADH is turned off when plasma osmolality falls below ~285 mOsm/kg. In SIADH, that off-switch is broken. ADH keeps pumping water in, expanding total body water. But the retained water stays *inside* the vascular space and interstitial fluid—the patient becomes “diluted” but not volume-overloaded because the body senses the volume expansion (despite normal measured volume) and suppresses aldosterone, so retained water is gradually shunted to urine. The key: **euvolemia** – the patient does not develop edema because the retained water escapes into the intracellular space as cells swell (that's why brain symptoms occur).

**Mechanism → sign mapping:**

- **Low plasma osmolality, high urine osmolality:** This is the “trump card” – normally, low plasma osm should trigger a dilute urine (urine osm <100). A urine osm >100 in the setting of low plasma osm means ADH is present when it shouldn't be. The higher the urine osm, the more inappropriate the ADH action.
- **High urine sodium:** Because the expanded volume suppresses the renin-aldosterone system, the kidney *doesn't try to hold onto sodium*. Any sodium you eat is excreted freely. So urine Na >20-40 mmol/L is typical. If urine Na is low (<20), think hypovolemia (kidneys clinging to Na).
- **Low serum uric acid and low BUN:** Chronic water retention dilutes uric acid and BUN. Uric acid <4 mg/dL is a sensitive marker for SIADH (normalization after correction suggests SIADH was not volume-depleted).
- **Absence of peripheral edema:** The retained water distributes evenly across all compartments – intracellular swelling causes neurological symptoms but extracellular volume doesn't stretch enough to cause pitting edema (unlike heart failure or cirrhosis where Starling forces are abnormal).

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette clues (euvolemia, high urine osm, high urine Na, low uric acid) all stem from this single pathophysiological story: inappropriate ADH → water retention → dilution → volume expansion → aldosterone suppression.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Pulmonary** | Small cell lung cancer (most common cause overall) | Heavy smoker, hilar mass on CXR |
| | Pneumonia, TB, lung abscess | Productive cough, fever, lobar consolidation |
| | Positive pressure ventilation | ICU patient on ventilator |
| **CNS** | Meningitis (bacterial/viral) | Headache, nuchal rigidity, CSF pleocytosis |
| | Stroke (especially hemorrhagic) | Sudden focal deficit, CT shows bleed |
| | Head trauma, brain tumor | History of fall, CT/mass |
| | Guillain-Barré | Ascending paralysis, recent infection |
| **Drugs** | SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine) | Starting or dose increase of antidepressant |
| | Carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine | Epilepsy or trigeminal neuralgia patient |
| | Vinca alkaloids (vincristine) | Chemotherapy, lymphoma patient |
| | Cyclophosphamide (high dose) | Pre–bone marrow transplant |
| | Desmopressin (DDAVP) | Patient on synthetic ADH for diabetes insipidus or enuresis |
| **Other** | Pain, stress, nausea (any cause) | Post-operative, severe pain, vomiting |
| | Idiopathic (no cause found, often elderly) | Exhaustive workup negative; may be due to reset osmostat |
| | HIV (multiple mechanisms) | HIV positive with low CD4, central lesions |

**Most common cause:** Small cell lung cancer (ectopic ADH). If a patient with SIADH is older, smoker, and has a lung lesion, assume SCLC until proven otherwise.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (Bartter-Schwartz, modified):**  
Essential for confirming SIADH. You need all of:
1. Hyponatremia (Na <135 mmol/L)
2. Low plasma osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg)
3. Inappropriately high urine osmolality (>100 mOsm/kg, often >plasma osm)
4. Elevated urine sodium (>20-40 mmol/L on normal salt intake)
5. Clinical euvolemia (no edema, no signs of hypovolemia)
6. Normal adrenal/thyroid/renal function (exclude mimics)

**Key tests and interpretation:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Serum osmolality** | True osmotic state | Differentiates hypo-osmolar from iso/hyperosmolar hyponatremia (pseudohyponatremia) | <275 mOsm/kg = true hyponatremia |
| **Urine osmolality** | Renal concentrating ability | Confirms inappropriate ADH action | >100 mOsm/kg with low plasma osm = SIADH; <100 = primary polydipsia or beer potomania |
| **Urine sodium** (spot) | Tubular sodium handling | Distinguishes SIADH from hypovolemia | >20-40 = SIADH, <20 = hypovolemic (or low salt intake) |
| **Serum uric acid** | Water dilution effect | Sensitive marker; low in SIADH, normal/high in hypovolemia | <4 mg/dL supports SIADH; normalizes after correction if SIADH |
| **Cortisol (AM or ACTH stimulation)** | Exclude adrenal insufficiency | Adrenal crisis can mimic SIADH; also causes low Na | Random cortisol <100 nmol/L or failed synACTHen test → adrenal insufficiency |
| **TSH (T4 if needed)** | Exclude hypothyroidism | Severe myxedema can cause hyponatremia | Elevated TSH, low T4 → treat thyroid |
| **Chest imaging** | Identify pulmonary cause | Rule out lung cancer, infection | Hilar mass → SCLC; infiltrate → pneumonia |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms / rules out**

- **Confirms SIADH:** Low plasma osm (<275) **plus** urine osm >100 **plus** euvolemia (with normal cortisol and TSH). Basically, if the urine is concentrated when the blood is dilute, you've nailed it.
- **Rules out SIADH:** Urine osmolality <100 mOsm/kg (thumbs up for primary polydipsia). Or if correction of hyponatremia with 0.9% saline leads to complete resolution, it's hypovolemic (not SIADH).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – treat symptoms, not numbers:**

- **Severe symptoms (seizures, GCS <13, severe confusion):**  
   → **3% hypertonic saline** 2 mL/kg IV bolus (e.g., 100-150 mL) over 10-20 minutes. [Some guidelines use 150 mL over 15 min].  
   → Goal: raise Na by **4-6 mmol/L** in first 4 hours – enough to reverse brain swelling, but not enough to risk osmotic demyelination.  
   → Check Na after each bolus; repeat bolus if symptoms persist and rise <4-6 mmol/L.  
   → **Why?** 3% NaCl is ~513 mmol/L of sodium. It draws water out of swollen brain cells, reducing intracranial pressure quickly.  
   → **Pitfall:** Don't aim for normal Na (~135) immediately – that's dangerous. Get safe, not normal.

- **Mild-moderate symptoms (headache, nausea, mild confusion):**  
   → **Fluid restriction** (first-line for asymptomatic/mild SIADH): limit to 800-1000 mL/day total fluid intake (all sources).  
   → Not a rapid fix (rises 1-2 mmol/L/day), but safe.

**Ward (24-72h) – gradual correction, avoid demyelination:**

- **Monitor Na q6-8h.** Target correction rate:  
   - ≤10 mmol/L in first 24h  
   - ≤18 mmol/L in first 48h (some say ≤8 mmol/L per 24h in high-risk patients).  
- **If rise too fast:** Consider desmopressin (DDAVP) 1-2 mcg IV/SC to “stop the free water loss” and re-lower Na. Yes, you read that right – you can treat overcorrection by giving ADH back!  
- **Continued fluid restriction** if Na slowly improving.  
- **If no response or refractory (fluid restriction fails after 48-72h):**  
   → **Demeclocycline** 300-600 mg PO BID (tetracycline that induces nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; takes 3-5 days to work).  
   → **Vasopressin receptor antagonist (vaptans):** Tolvaptan 15 mg PO daily, titrate up to 30-60 mg. Use only if serum Na <125 and patient has no liver disease (risk of hepatotoxicity). Warn: can cause overcorrection – must monitor every 4-6 hours.  
   → **Why vaptans?** They block ADH at V2 receptor, causing aquaporin downregulation and water diuresis. Expensive, narrow indication.

**Long-term – treat the cause:**

- **If underlying drug** (SSRI, carbamazepine, etc.) → stop or switch (with advice from pharmacist/specialist).  
- **If SCLC** → chemo (resolution of hyponatremia often parallels tumor response). Temporarily use fluid restriction or vaptans.  
- **If idiopathic/reset osmostat** → long-term fluid restriction, possibly fludrocortisone (but risk of edema/hypertension).

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do NOT give 0.9% normal saline as first line in SIADH** – the sodium load (154 mmol/L) is excreted, but the water is retained, so hyponatremia may *worsen* in urine-concentrating states. Exception: if hypovolemia is part of the picture (mixed SIADH + volume depletion).  
- **Do NOT correct by >10 mmol/L in 24h** – osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) is permanent. High-risk: alcoholics, liver disease, malnourished, K+ <4.  
- **Do NOT immediately think “fluid restriction failed” after 12 hours** – it takes 24-48h to see effect. Be patient.  
- **Do NOT use vaptans in hypoalbuminemia, liver disease, or anuric kidney failure.**

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | SIADH | Cerebral Salt Wasting (CSW) | Hypovolemic Hyponatremia | Primary Polydipsia | Thiazide-Induced |
|---------|-------|----------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Volume status** | Euvolemic | Hypovolemic (low JVP, dry) | Hypovolemic | Euvolemic | Mild hypovolemia |
| **Urine sodium** | >20-40 | >20 (high, driven by natriuretic peptides) | <10-20 | >20 (but dilute urine) | Variable (often high due to Na loss) |
| **Urine osmolality** | >100 (often >300) | >100 | >100 (concentrating due to low volume) | <100 (dilute, <50) | >100 (if ADH also released) |
| **Serum uric acid** | Low (<4) | Low (also due to natriuresis) | Normal/high (volume contraction) | Low-normal | Variable |
| **Response to 0.9% saline** | No change or worsens | Corrects hyponatremia | Corrects hyponatremia | Corrects quickly | Corrects |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Euvolemia + high urine osm | Hypovolemia + high urine Na (often intracranial disease) | Low urine Na | Low urine osm (<100) | Recent thiazide start; resolves after stopping |

**Clinical pearl:** The hardest distinction is between SIADH and CSW in neurosurgical patients. A 2L NS challenge: if Na improves and volume normalizes, it's CSW. If Na drops or stays same, it's SIADH.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Brain swelling (cerebral edema) – acute hyponatremia**  
- **Presents:** Severe headache, vomiting, confusion, seizures, coma, respiratory arrest.  
- **Key signs:** GCS ≤12, focal neurology? (Think CVA vs. SIADH).  
- **What to do:** Hypertonic saline bolus (as above) and urgent senior review.

**2. Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) – from overcorrection**  
- **Presents:** Flaccid quadriparesis, dysarthria, dysphagia, locked-in state. Typically **2-5 days after correction**.  
- **Key signs:** Bilateral weakness + bulbar signs. MRI shows symmetric hyperintensities in pons (central pontine myelinolysis) or extrapontine.  
- **What to do:** Prevent! If overcorrection occurs (>10 mmol/L in 24h), give DDAVP 1-2 mcg IV/SQ to stop water loss, and consider re-lowering Na with D5W or free-water bolus (under expert guidance).  
- **Late-presentation pattern that tests “what if untreated”:** A chronic hyponatremic patient (Na 110-115) is corrected to 125 in 12 hours with high rate fluids → 5 days later they are bed-bound, mute, unable to swallow → ODS. Classic exam stem.

**3. Seizures**  
- From severe hyponatremia (Na <115). Treat with hypertonic saline + benzodiazepines. Avoid phenytoin if possible.

**4. Hypovolemia from hypertonic saline? (rare)**  
- Overly aggressive hypertonic saline can cause fluid overload if patient has reduced ejection fraction or renal failure. Monitor for pulmonary edema.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **SIADH causes: “I P 3 C”**  
  - **I** – Idiopathic  
  - **P** – Pulmonary (cancer, infection, vent)  
  - **3 C** – CNS (tumor, bleed, meningitis), Chemodrugs (vinca, cyclophosphamide), Common drugs (SSRI, carbamazepine)  

- **Diagnostic criteria: “L U N A R”**  
  - Low plasma osm (<275)  
  - Urine osm >100 (or >plasm osm)  
  - No edema (euvolemia)  
  - Acute? Check symptoms  
  - Renals normal – also check adrenal/thyroid  

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Uric acid is your shortcut:** If low (<4 mg/dL) in a hyponatremic patient, odds are SIADH. If normal or high, think hypovolemia or thiazides.
2. **BUN <10 mg/dL (in SI units <3.6 mmol/L)** also suggests water-diluted state.
3. **Never rely on urine Na alone** – in a low-salt diet, even SIADH can have low urine Na. Always interpret with volume status.
4. **If urine osm <100, stop everything – it's not SIADH.** It's polydipsia or beer potomania.
5. **The “stress triad”:** Post-op patient with pain, nausea, and opioids → high ADH. Often iatrogenic SIADH. Low threshold to restrict fluids.
6. **Check potassium before correcting Na** – if hypokalemic, correct K first because K deficit can mask total body Na deficit; you may overcorrect Na.
7. **In the elderly “reset osmostat” variant:** ADH turns on at a lower set point (e.g., plasma osm 260). Urine osm becomes dilute if plasma osm falls below that set point. Diagnose with water loading test (rarely needed). Treat with mild fluid restriction only.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking CSW for SIADH and fluid-restricting (causes hypovolemic shock).  
- Giving “normal saline” for SIADH as a routine – almost always wrong.  
- Discharging a patient with Na 128 and asymptomatic without plan – they may bounce back with seizures if they drink too much.  
- Failing to check cortisol in SIADH – missing adrenal insufficiency is a killable mistake.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / MET team:**

- **Symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, GCS <13, severe confusion)** – call ICU/high dependency for hypertonic saline infusion and monitoring.
- **Na <115 mmol/L** even if asymptomatic (high risk of seizure, needs monitored setting).
- **Correction rate >10 mmol/L in 24h** – call renal/endocrinology senior for guidance on DDAVP re-lowering.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** (especially if respiratory rate >25, O2 sats <91%, systolic BP <90) – escalate as per local policy.
- **Lactate >2** (if hypovolemic component or sepsis).
- **GCS drop ≥2 points** from baseline.
- **Any evidence of osmotic demyelination** (triad: dysarthria + quadriparesis + altered mental status days after correction) – urgent neurology/MRI.

**Specific thresholds to act on:**

- Na <120 + any neurological symptom → immediate hypertonic saline (see management)
- Na <125 and rapid fall (e.g., drop >0.5 mmol/L/hour) → consider ICU if symptomatic
- K+ <3.5 mmol/L in a hyponatremic patient (must correct K first to avoid overcorrection of Na)
- In patient on vaptans: check LFTs before starting and at 1 week; if ALT >3x ULN, stop vaptan and call senior.
